perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given (a) when delivered in person, (b) five (5) business days after being sent by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, (c) when dispatched by electronic facsimile transmission (with confirmation of successful transmission), or (d) one (1) business day after having been dispatched by an internationally recognized overnight courier service, in each case to the appropriate Party at the address or facsimile number specified below:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given (a) when delivered in person, (b) fifteen (15) business days after being sent by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, (c) when dispatched by electronic facsimile transmission (with confirmation of successful transmission), or (d) five (5) business days after having been dispatched by an internationally recognized overnight courier service, in each case to the appropriate Party at the address or facsimile number specified below:
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the registered mail notice period to 15 business days could create issues if other deadlines in the contract are tied to receipt of notice and are shorter than that timeframe, potentially violating regulations requiring timely notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: While NRS 104.1201 defines 'notice' and 'notification' in the context of the Uniform Commercial Code as taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person, making the notice period too long can conflict with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Nevada law. By extending the notice period to 15 days, it can unduly delay other contractual obligations.

perturbation.justification: The modification extends the notice period for registered mail to 15 business days, which can create a legal contradiction. The LLM explanation suggests this delay may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if it delays other contractual obligations. NRS 104.1304 mentions the 'Obligation of good faith'. The original text provided a shorter timeframe. While the provided snippet doesn't define 'good faith,' the extended delay could be interpreted as not acting in good faith if it hinders timely performance of other contractual duties. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition states that contradictions arise when 'time-sensitive obligations' are not aligned with legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties may terminate its performance of related obligations under this Agreement within thirty (30) days of receipt by the Party of written termination notice.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may terminate its performance of related obligations under this Agreement within ten (10) days of receipt by the Party of written termination notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days might conflict with state laws requiring longer notice periods for contract terminations, especially if the agreement implicitly involves employment or has significant economic consequences for either party.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 597.170 requires compliance with federal statutes and regulations related to consumer contracts and cancellation rights. Reducing the notice period for termination to ten days may conflict with federal laws or regulations designed to protect parties entering contracts, especially if termination involves the sale of goods or services covered by federal consumer protection acts, making the ten-day period unreasonably short.

perturbation.justification: The original text provides for a 30-day termination notice, while the changed text reduces this to 10 days. The Law Explanation suggests this reduction might conflict with federal laws or regulations related to consumer contracts and cancellation rights, especially if termination involves the sale of goods or services. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically NRS 597.170 and NRS 597.175, indicates that terms of a contract that alter or waive provisions may be void and unenforceable, implying that reducing a mandated notice period could be considered an alteration that contradicts the law.
